text: The surgeon observes that the transverse colon appears adhered to the descending colon, making it difficult to visually distinguish between the two sections.
The surgeon performs lateral dissection around the splenic flexure, attempting to clarify the anatomical orientation and relationships of the colon segments.
The surgeon visually examines the area, identifying the structure as the transverse colon.
The omentum is visible on the distal transverse colon, with its fatty tissue draped over the colon segment.
The surgeon begins to dissect and separate the transverse colon from the descending colon, which appear adhered together.
The surgeon attempts to mobilize the splenic flexure in the left upper quadrant, while observing and assessing the surrounding anatomical structures.
The surgeon performs a lateral dissection to medialize the bowel, working around anatomical challenges that prevented a sub-IMV approach.

The surgeon attempts to identify the mesentery more clearly, noting the tattoo in the proximal descending colon. The flexure is being taken down, with visible bowel in the left upper quadrant that appears unclear.
The surgeon examines the attachment between the transverse colon and the descending colon, focusing on the area marked by a tattoo.
The surgeon observes the mesentery of the bowel positioned below the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies a hernia defect beneath the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) while manipulating surrounding fat tissue, clarifying that the connection is not mesentery.
The surgeon observes that almost all of the small bowel is positioned in the upper left quadrant of the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon observes the bowel located above the splenic flexure in the left upper quadrant, noting its position relative to the surrounding anatomical structures.
The surgeon observes a large hernia defect beneath the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) in the surgical field.
The surgeon attempts to reduce the bowel contents from the left upper quadrant, aiming to visualize the ligament of Treitz.
The surgeon observes the splenic flexure positioned downward in the left upper quadrant.
The surgeon attempts to separate the transverse colon from the descending colon, carefully navigating around congenital attachments to preserve as much bowel as possible.
The surgeon observes a large defect in the surgical field, likely related to the previously mentioned congenital attachments.
The surgeon identifies and begins to take down the remaining lateral attachments to fully straighten the bowel.
The surgeon applies medial tension to the colon mesentery and continues dissection over the top of the kidney, aiming to separate the mesentery from surrounding structures.
The surgeon identifies the left ureter visible in the distance, amidst the ongoing dissection of the colon mesentery.
The surgeon addresses the final lateral attachments of the descending colon, extending towards the splenic flexure.
The surgeon identifies the ligament of Treitz positioned laterally in the surgical field.
The transverse colon momentum is visible at the distal transverse colon, showing its anatomical position and relationship to surrounding structures.
The surgeon continues to dissect and take down the remaining lateral attachments of the descending colon near the flexure.
With the lateral attachments released, the surgeon observes the proper orientation of the colon, ensuring clear visualization of the anatomical structures.
The surgeon performs further lateral mobilization to release tension from the lateral attachments of the descending colon, ensuring complete detachment from surrounding structures.
The surgeon attempts to identify the distal margin of resection, focusing on the area near the proximal descending colon where the tumor is located.
The surgeon observes the sigmoid colon as it descends into the pelvis, noting the presence of unusual and robust lateral adhesions to the pelvic sidewall.
The surgeon has mobilized the colon and is preparing for the mesenteric transaction, focusing on planning the proximal and distal margins for resection.
The surgeon prepares to transect the mesentery at the proximal colon, indicating the initial step in the planned resection approach.
The surgeon is working in the area below the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV), focusing on the sub-IMV region during the colectomy procedure.
The surgeon uses their left hand to hold the proximal margin of the colon while dissecting the mesentery directly along the chosen margin.
The surgeon has not extensively dissected the blood vessels in the current view, focusing instead on preparing the mesentery for transaction.
The surgeon observes thickened tissues, likely due to chronic friction from bowel herniation beneath the mesentery.
The surgeon begins dissection of the distal part of the colon, focusing on isolating the left colic artery from surrounding tissues.

The surgeon carefully preserves the superior hemorrhoidal artery while dissecting around the splenic flexure. The inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) is visible along the aorta as the dissection progresses beneath the superior hemorrhoidal artery.
The surgeon prepares to take the left colic artery off of the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) as part of the splenic flexure resection.
The surgeon dissects the tissue while tracing the left colic artery upwards, revealing the anatomical structures along its path.

The surgeon identifies the superior hemorrhoidal artery descending and the left colic artery positioned superior to it. The left colic artery is dissected free, preparing for sealing with a vessel sealer.
The surgeon identifies and exposes sigmoidal branches emerging from the dissected area near the left colic artery.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to take the left colic branch, tracing it directly up to the chosen distal margin located in the mid sigmoid colon.
The surgeon places a twelve millimeter trocar as the third arm and relocates the camera to the second port for better visualization during the procedure.
The surgeon prepares to perform a sewn anastomosis following the division of the bowel.
The surgeon uses two OPDS devices to join the two staple lines, aligning the edges of the bowel segments for anastomosis.
The camera is repositioned to port number three, providing a new visual perspective of the surgical field.
The surgeon aligns and joins two staple lines, ensuring they come together smoothly in the surgical field.
The surgeon ties the suture to itself, securing the closure of the surgical site.
The surgeon has completed the colotomies and checks the indocyanine green (ICG) fluorescence, which appears optimal.
The surgeon performs the inner layer closure using three OV lock sutures, completing the suturing process within the surgical field.
The surgeon begins the second suture in the middle of the surgical field, running it down towards the camera view.
The surgeon brings two sutures together over the top in a canal fashion, aligning them precisely to secure the tissue.
The surgeon ties the sutures together, completing the stitching process over the colotomies.
The surgeon places the outer anterior row of sutures using 2-0 PDS, securing the tissue layers in the surgical field.
The surgeon completes a two-layer anastomosis, visually finalizing the suturing process with the outer and inner layers securely in place.
The surgeon prepares to extract the bowel from the pelvic cavity, positioning the specimen for removal.
The surgeon inserts an EEA sizer into the vagina, preparing for a transvaginal extraction of the specimen.
The surgeon performs a vaginotomy, creating an incision in the vaginal wall to facilitate the transvaginal extraction of the bowel specimen.
The surgeon introduces a fifteen millimeter endocatch bag into the surgical field, preparing for the transvaginal extraction of the bowel segment.
The surgeon extracts cancerous specimens from the surgical field and places them into a 15mm endocatch bag for containment.
The surgeon closes the endocatch bag containing the extracted specimens and removes it from the surgical field.
The surgeon opens the endocatch bag outside the body, grasps one of the staple lines, allowing the bowel to unfold and be extracted smoothly.
The surgeon examines the small vaginotomy, noting that it has not torn or stretched significantly, which can occur in diverticular cases.
The surgeon closes the opening of the endocatch bag after placing the specimen inside.
The surgeon performs a closure involving the mucosa and muscular layer of the bowel.
The surgeon closes the peritoneum by running sutures through the open peritoneal layer, covering the underlying structures.
The surgeon performs a leak test on the anastomosis, which is located closer to the flexure than the pelvis, taking advantage of the laxity and lack of tension in the bowel.
The surgeon performs a final inspection of the surgical field, ensuring all structures appear intact and there are no signs of complications.
The surgeon visually inspects the mobilized splenic flexure, which has been freed from its attachments during the colectomy procedure.
The surgeon visually identifies the ligament of Treitz, noting its unusual appearance.
